questions.text: Mahatma Gandhi returned to India from South Africa in:
questions.explanation: After successfully leading a Satyagraha movement in South Africa, Gandhi returned to India in 1915.

questions.text: The idea of Satyagraha emphasized the power of:
questions.explanation: Satyagraha means 'truth-force'; it emphasizes the power of truth and the need to search for truth.

questions.text: Where did Gandhi launch the first Satyagraha movement in India?
questions.explanation: In 1917, Gandhi traveled to Champaran, Bihar, to inspire peasants to struggle against the oppressive indigo plantation system.

questions.text: The Rowlatt Act was passed by the Imperial Legislative Council in:
questions.explanation: Despite the opposition of Indian members, the Act was passed in 1919, allowing the government to detain political prisoners without trial.

questions.text: When did the Jallianwala Bagh massacre take place?
questions.explanation: On the day of Baisakhi, General Dyer opened fire on a peaceful crowd gathered in Jallianwala Bagh.

questions.text: Who was responsible for the Jallianwala Bagh incident?
questions.explanation: General Dyer blocked the exit points and ordered his troops to fire on the crowd to 'produce a moral effect'.

questions.text: The Khilafat Committee was formed in Bombay in 1919 to support the Sultan of:
questions.explanation: The Ottoman Sultan (Khalifa) was the spiritual head of the Islamic world, and Indian Muslims formed the committee to defend his powers.

questions.text: Who wrote the book 'Hind Swaraj'?
questions.explanation: In 1909, Gandhi declared in 'Hind Swaraj' that British rule survived in India only because of the cooperation of Indians.

questions.text: The Non-Cooperation-Khilafat Movement began in:
questions.explanation: The movement was formally launched in January 1921 after being adopted at the Nagpur Congress session.

questions.text: The incident that led to the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement was:
questions.explanation: In February 1922, a violent clash at Chauri Chaura led Gandhi to call off the movement as it was turning violent.

questions.text: Who were the founders of the Swaraj Party?
questions.explanation: C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru formed the party within Congress to argue for a return to council politics.

questions.text: The Simon Commission was boycotted in India because:
questions.explanation: The statutory commission sent to suggest constitutional changes in India consisted entirely of British members.

questions.text: The demand for 'Purna Swaraj' (Full Independence) was formalized in the Congress session at:
questions.explanation: In December 1929, under the presidency of Jawaharlal Nehru, the Lahore Congress formalized the demand for Purna Swaraj.

questions.text: Mahatma Gandhi started the Salt March from Sabarmati to:
questions.explanation: The 240-mile march ended at the coastal town of Dandi, where Gandhi broke the salt law.

questions.text: How many volunteers accompanied Gandhi on the Salt March?
questions.explanation: Gandhi started the march with 78 of his trusted volunteers.

questions.text: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact was signed in:
questions.explanation: Signed on 5 March 1931, Gandhi agreed to participate in the Round Table Conference and call off the Civil Disobedience Movement.

questions.text: Who organized the Dalits into the Depressed Classes Association in 1930?
questions.explanation: Dr. Ambedkar championed the cause of Dalits and clashed with Gandhi over separate electorates.

questions.text: The Poona Pact of September 1932 was signed between:
questions.explanation: The pact gave reserved seats to the Depressed Classes in provincial and central councils instead of separate electorates.

questions.text: Who painted the first image of 'Bharat Mata'?
questions.explanation: Abanindranath Tagore portrayed Bharat Mata as an ascetic figure, calm, composed, divine, and spiritual.

questions.text: Who composed the song 'Vande Mataram'?
questions.explanation: It was written in the 1870s as a hymn to the motherland and later included in the novel 'Anandamath'.

questions.text: The Inland Emigration Act of 1859 was harsh for:
questions.explanation: Under this act, plantation workers in Assam were not permitted to leave the tea gardens without permission.

questions.text: What was the main reason for the growth of nationalism in the colonies?
questions.explanation: The struggle against colonial rule provided a shared bond that united different groups.

questions.text: The First World War created a new economic and political situation in India by:
options: Introducing income tax and increasing customs duties
questions.explanation: To finance war expenditures, the British government increased taxes and introduced income tax in India.

questions.text: Which movement was launched against the Salt tax?
questions.explanation: The Salt March marked the beginning of the Civil Disobedience Movement.

questions.text: Who led the peasant movement in Awadh?
questions.explanation: Baba Ramchandra, a sanyasi, led peasants against high rents and 'begar' (unpaid labor).

questions.text: What was the significance of 26 January 1930?
questions.explanation: The Lahore Congress declared 26 Jan 1930 as Independence Day to be celebrated annually.

questions.text: Alluri Sitarama Raju is known for leading a tribal movement in:
questions.explanation: He led a militant guerrilla movement against colonial forest laws.

questions.text: The 'Oudh Kisan Sabha' was headed by:
questions.explanation: It was set up in 1920 to integrate the Awadh peasant struggle with the national movement.

questions.text: Why did the industrial working classes stay away from the Civil Disobedience Movement, except in Nagpur?
questions.explanation: As Congress became closer to industrialist groups, workers felt sidelined.

questions.text: The Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI) was formed in:
questions.explanation: Indian merchants and industrialists formed FICCI to organize their business interests and oppose colonial policies.

questions.text: Who led the Muslim League and demanded separate electorates for Muslims?
questions.explanation: Jinnah was a prominent leader who argued for separate electorates to protect Muslim interests.

questions.text: Folklore and tales were used by nationalists to:
options: Restore a sense of pride in one's past
questions.explanation: Nationalists believed that folk traditions provided a true picture of traditional culture that had been damaged by outsiders.

questions.text: Who was known as the 'Frontier Gandhi'?
questions.explanation: A devout disciple of Gandhi, he led the movement in Peshawar.

questions.text: In the Gudem Hills, Raju claimed he had special powers like:
questions.explanation: Tribals believed he was an incarnation of God due to his claimed powers.

questions.text: What was 'begar'?
options: System of forced labor without payment
questions.explanation: Begar was a practice where peasants were forced to work at landlords' farms without any wages.

questions.text: Which event officially marked the beginning of the Civil Disobedience Movement?
questions.explanation: On 6 April 1930, Gandhi ceremonially violated the law by manufacturing salt.

questions.text: The tricolour flag designed during the Swadeshi movement in Bengal had which colours?
questions.explanation: The early Swadeshi flag featured eight lotuses representing eight provinces of British India.

questions.text: The Khilafat movement was organized by:
questions.explanation: The Ali brothers led the movement to defend the temporal powers of the Khalifa.

questions.text: Natesa Sastri published a massive four-volume collection of Tamil folk tales called:
questions.explanation: He believed folklore was national literature and 'the most trustworthy manifestation of people's real thoughts'.

questions.text: Gandhi's spinning wheel in the center of the Swaraj flag (1921) represented:
questions.explanation: The charkha represented self-reliance and the economic independence of the masses.
